Overview

This notebook extends the earlier heart-rate project by using Apple Health’s recorded Resting Heart Rate metric directly instead of estimating resting heart rate from the lowest 20 percent of daily heart-rate readings.

That change makes the analysis easier to explain and more aligned with the metric Apple Health itself reports. The workflow still keeps an important rule from the earlier analysis: missing days are not filled in, so the trend is based only on days with observed data.

In [1]:
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
import pandas as pd
In [2]:
import os
os.getcwd()
Out[2]:
'C:\\Users\\arivu'
In [3]:
tree = ET.parse('export.xml')
root = tree.getroot()
In [4]:
records = []

for record in root.findall('Record'):
    records.append({
        "type": record.get('type'),
        "startDate": record.get('startDate'),
        "endDate": record.get('endDate'),
        "value": record.get('value'),
        "unit": record.get('unit'),
        "sourceName": record.get('sourceName'),
        "sourceVersion": record.get('sourceVersion')
    })

df = pd.DataFrame(records)
df.head()

Extracting Resting Heart Rate

The dataset contains 72 Apple Health record types, so the first task is to isolate the Resting Heart Rate records. This step creates a focused dataframe containing only the observations relevant to baseline cardiovascular trend analysis.

A quick preview confirms that the resting-heart-rate data contains timestamps, values, units, source names, and source versions, which makes it possible to study both timing and source-related differences.

In [9]:
rest_hr = df[df['type'] == 'HKQuantityTypeIdentifierRestingHeartRate']
In [10]:
rest_hr.head()
Out[10]:
type startDate endDate value unit sourceName sourceVersion
1851108 HKQuantityTypeIdentifierRestingHeartRate 2021-02-24 11:20:11 -0500 2021-02-24 11:20:38 -0500 95 count/min Withings 5040011
1851109 HKQuantityTypeIdentifierRestingHeartRate 2021-02-24 07:45:56 -0500 2021-02-24 07:46:18 -0500 78 count/min Withings 5040011
1851110 HKQuantityTypeIdentifierRestingHeartRate 2021-02-24 03:00:48 -0500 2021-02-24 03:01:10 -0500 90 count/min Withings 5040011
1851111 HKQuantityTypeIdentifierRestingHeartRate 2021-02-24 13:55:41 -0500 2021-02-24 13:56:03 -0500 118 count/min Withings 5040011
1851112 HKQuantityTypeIdentifierRestingHeartRate 2021-02-24 11:29:42 -0500 2021-02-24 11:30:11 -0500 88 count/min Withings 5040011

In [14]:
rest_hr=rest_hr.copy()

Data Cleaning and Type Conversion

The raw resting-heart-rate records initially load as text values, so the timestamps are converted to datetime and the heart-rate values are converted to numeric format. Timezone information is removed to make date-based aggregation simpler and more consistent within the notebook.

After conversion, invalid values are dropped. This keeps the analysis from being distorted by malformed records while still preserving the original missingness pattern.

In [15]:
rest_hr['startDate'] = pd.to_datetime(rest_hr['startDate'], errors='coerce')
rest_hr['endDate']   = pd.to_datetime(rest_hr['endDate'], errors='coerce')
In [16]:
rest_hr['startDate'] = rest_hr['startDate'].dt.tz_localize(None)
rest_hr['endDate']   = rest_hr['endDate'].dt.tz_localize(None)
In [17]:
rest_hr['value'] = pd.to_numeric(rest_hr['value'], errors='coerce')

Resting Heart Rate Summary Statistics

The descriptive summary shows the size and spread of the resting-heart-rate dataset. In this notebook, nearly four thousand resting-heart-rate records are available, with a median in the mid-80s and values ranging from 41 to 194 beats per minute.

These summary statistics show that the dataset is large enough for long-term trend analysis, but also variable enough that aggregation is useful before drawing conclusions.

In [18]:
rest_hr['value'].describe()
Out[18]:
count    3989.000000
mean       86.173477
std        14.379353
min        41.000000
25%        77.000000
50%        84.000000
75%        94.000000
max       194.000000
Name: value, dtype: float64
In [19]:
rest_hr = rest_hr.dropna(subset=['value', 'startDate'])

Daily and Monthly Aggregation

Apple Health may record multiple resting-heart-rate values on a given day. To create a stable daily series, the notebook takes the median resting heart rate for each day. That daily series is then aggregated to a monthly median to reduce noise and make broader shifts easier to see.

This two-level aggregation keeps the project grounded in Apple’s direct resting-heart-rate metric while still producing a trend series suitable for visualization and change-point analysis.

In [20]:
rest_hr['date'] = rest_hr['startDate'].dt.date

daily_rest_hr = (
    rest_hr.groupby('date')['value']
    .median()
    .reset_index(name='resting_hr')
)

daily_rest_hr['date'] = pd.to_datetime(daily_rest_hr['date'])
In [24]:
monthly_rest = (
    daily_rest_hr.groupby(daily_rest_hr['date'].dt.to_period('M'))['resting_hr']
    .median()
    .reset_index()
)

monthly_rest['date'] = monthly_rest['date'].dt.to_timestamp()

monthly_rest.plot(x='date', y='resting_hr')
Out[24]:
<AxesSubplot:xlabel='date'>
In [25]:
daily_rest_hr['year'] = daily_rest_hr['date'].dt.year
daily_rest_hr['day_of_year'] = daily_rest_hr['date'].dt.dayofyear

Yearly Plots and Before/After Metformin View

The notebook then plots the daily resting-heart-rate trend separately for each year, including an average reference line within each yearly chart. This makes it easier to compare how the baseline shifts across calendar years rather than viewing the entire timeline as a single dense line.

A separate before-and-after split around August 2025 is also used to explore the time period when metformin was introduced. This does not prove causation, but it provides a structured way to visually compare earlier and later phases of the resting-heart-rate trend.

In [26]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

for year, group in daily_rest_hr.groupby('year'):
    plt.figure(figsize=(18, 8))   # ✅ create figure first
    group = group.sort_values('date')
    avg = group['resting_hr'].mean()
    
    plt.plot(group['date'], group['resting_hr'], label='Daily Resting HR')
    plt.axhline(avg, linestyle='--', label=f'Avg: {avg:.1f}')
    
    plt.grid(True)
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    
    plt.title(f"Resting HR - {year}")
    plt.xlabel("Date")
    plt.ylabel("Resting HR")
    plt.legend()
    
    plt.show()
In [27]:
cutoff = pd.to_datetime("2025-08-01")
In [28]:
before_metformin = daily_rest_hr[daily_rest_hr['date'] < cutoff]
after_metformin  = daily_rest_hr[daily_rest_hr['date'] >= cutoff]
In [29]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

for label, data in {
    "Before Metformin": before_metformin,
    "After Metformin": after_metformin
}.items():
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(14,6))
    
    data = data.sort_values('date')
    avg = data['resting_hr'].mean()
    
    plt.plot(data['date'], data['resting_hr'], label='Resting HR')
    plt.axhline(avg, linestyle='--', label=f'Avg: {avg:.1f}')
    
    plt.title(label)
    plt.legend()
    plt.grid(True)
    plt.savefig(f"{label.replace(' ', '_')}.png", bbox_inches='tight')
    plt.show()

Hourly Resting Heart Rate Pattern

The analysis also extracts the hour from each resting-heart-rate timestamp and groups the observations by hour of day. This provides a simple view of how recorded resting heart rate varies across a 24-hour cycle.

This helps add time-of-day context to the project. Lower values are generally more likely during sleep or low-activity periods, while higher recorded resting values may appear during waking hours depending on how Apple Health captured the metric.

In [31]:
rest_hr['hour'] = rest_hr['startDate'].dt.hour
In [32]:
hourly = rest_hr.groupby('hour')['value'].mean().reset_index()
In [33]:
import os
os.makedirs("images", exist_ok=True)
In [34]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))
plt.plot(hourly['hour'], hourly['value'], marker='o')

plt.title("Average Heart Rate by Hour of Day")
plt.xlabel("Hour (0–23)")
plt.ylabel("Heart Rate")

plt.grid(True)
plt.xticks(range(0,24))

plt.savefig("images/hourly_hr.png", bbox_inches='tight')  # optional
plt.show()

Change Point Detection

To identify when the long-term resting-heart-rate baseline shifted, the monthly series is analyzed using rolling averages before and after each point. The absolute difference between those windows is used as a change score.

A minimum spacing rule of about 180 days is then used when selecting major change points. This prevents the chart from marking multiple nearby dates that represent the same local transition.

In [35]:
cp = monthly_rest.copy().sort_values('date')
cp = cp.dropna(subset=['resting_hr'])

cp.head()
Out[35]:
date resting_hr
0 2021-02-01 85.00
1 2021-03-01 88.75
2 2021-04-01 86.75
3 2021-05-01 72.00
4 2021-06-01 79.00
In [36]:
cp['prev_3'] = cp['resting_hr'].rolling(3).mean().shift(1)
cp['next_3'] = cp['resting_hr'].rolling(3).mean().shift(-2)

cp['change_score'] = (cp['next_3'] - cp['prev_3']).abs()
In [37]:
cp_sorted = cp.sort_values('change_score', ascending=False)
In [38]:
min_gap = 180

selected = []
for _, row in cp_sorted.iterrows():
    if all(abs((row['date'] - s['date']).days) > min_gap for s in selected):
        selected.append(row)
    if len(selected) == 3:
        break

top_points = pd.DataFrame(selected)
In [39]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import os

# ensure folder exists
os.makedirs("images", exist_ok=True)

plt.figure(figsize=(16,8))

# main trend
plt.plot(cp['date'], cp['resting_hr'], linewidth=2, label='Monthly Resting HR')

# plot change points with annotations
for i, (_, row) in enumerate(top_points.iterrows()):
    plt.axvline(row['date'], linestyle='--', linewidth=1.5, color='gray')
    plt.scatter(row['date'], row['resting_hr'], s=100)
    
    plt.annotate(
        row['date'].strftime('%Y-%m'),
        (row['date'], row['resting_hr']),
        textcoords="offset points",
        xytext=(0, 12 + i*10),   # stagger labels
        ha='center',
        fontsize=10,
        arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->')
    )

# labels and styling
plt.title("Resting Heart Rate Trend with Change Points", fontsize=16)
plt.xlabel("Date")
plt.ylabel("Resting HR")

plt.grid(True)
plt.legend()

# save image
plt.savefig("images/final_change_points.png", bbox_inches='tight')

# show plot
plt.show()

Conclusion

This notebook shows a stronger version of the earlier Apple Health heart-rate analysis by switching from an estimated resting-heart-rate proxy to Apple Health’s direct Resting Heart Rate metric. That makes the methodology simpler, more interpretable, and more defensible.

The cleaned resting-heart-rate data was aggregated into daily and monthly series, visualized by year, compared across a metformin-related time split, examined by hour of day, and analyzed with change-point detection. Together, these steps suggest that baseline resting heart rate changed over time in phases rather than through one abrupt jump.

Overall, this project demonstrates a practical end-to-end workflow for wearable health data: filtering mixed health records, cleaning timestamps and numeric values, preserving missingness correctly, building interpretable trend summaries, and using visual plus statistical methods to detect structural changes in a longitudinal physiological metric.
